description: Symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. Scenario: This issue can occur after resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move from one VSX device to the other, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Using the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

description: Symptom: Rules with workload groups configured in security policy applied on a VLAN are not hit even if there is a valid match. Scenario: If a host is connected to VSX pair, the hose is determined as local to both DSS in VSX pair in two ways: 1. PSM looks at LLDP information reported by the host (in case of multihomed environment, both DSS are considered local to the host). Host to DSS association is realized through this. 2. PSM looks at VSX information to determine the local VSX. In a multihomed environment, any one of the above two is sufficient to determine the local DSS(s) for a given host. In a singlehomed environment, PSM depends on (2) as (1) does not have LLDP of host for both DSS. For this issue, the host is single homed and there was an issue with (2). This resulted in PSM determining a852.d4f7.2a80 as remote DSS for orch2--vm-388646.

description: Symptom: Classifier Policy is not enforced for traffic coming from an ISL link. Scenario: For a topology with VSX+ VRRP, if the policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting classifier policy enforced. Even though the policy action is PBR redirect, those packets are not getting redirected. Workaround: Two workarounds are possible to prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL. 1. Use active gateway to give better active active redundancy. Packets reaching either VSX device process the packet destined to active gateway. 2. Turn off the interface connection from VRRP secondary device to down switch. This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

description: Symptom: Traffic disruption occurs after globally removing a VLAN associated with both a VLAN Translation rule and VXLAN interface. Scenario: This issue occurs when following these steps: 1. Create a VLAN and associating to VXLAN and a host connected port. 2. On a host connected port, configure VLAN translate 210 to 410. 3. Then delete VLAN 410 globally. Workaround: Reconfigure vlan trunk allow 210 on a host connected port.

description: Symptom: BGP is interjecting default route to all switches, and intermittently the BGP internal route incorrectly takes precedence over a similar BGP external route. Scenario: This issue may occur in any instance where an internal and external BGP route have the same attributes. description: Symptom: Querier information is not learnt on non-querier devices. Scenario: PSM needs to be configured. With this lag521 and lag522 are created internally and assigned to the VLAN with PSM. With this, when flooding the query packet to all the ports of the VLAN, the send is failing for the internal lag ports and skipping the rest of the ports instead of processing them. Workaround: Remove the PSM configuration.

section: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
task: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
procedure: Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, DL.10.xx.yyyy).
Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config.
Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

messages.content: For 10000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?

Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, DL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

procedure: Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.

Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.

When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
